subject: Craig Federighi
evidence_standard: L1 = verbatim from primary sources, L2 = reported/documented, L3 = inferred

education: BS Electrical Engineering and Computer Science, UC Berkeley, 1991
biography.title: Senior Vice President, Software Engineering, Apple Inc.

key_facts: Led development of the Enterprise Objects Framework — an early ORM for connecting objects to relational databases
NeXT's engineering culture: emphasis on object-oriented design, frameworks, tight layering of software — this became Apple's architecture after the 1997 acquisition
Joined Apple with the NeXT acquisition
Worked on early Mac OS X (Rhapsody) development — the integration of NeXT's OpenStep into Apple's platform
Formative period: learned how to transplant an engineering culture into an existing company and product line

career_timeline.role: Multiple roles including Chief Technology Officer
key_facts: Left Apple for enterprise software company Ariba
Enterprise software experience: scale, reliability, and B2B software quality expectations differ sharply from consumer software

career_timeline.role: VP Mac Software Engineering → SVP Software Engineering
key_facts: Returned to Apple in 2009 — reportedly at Steve Jobs's personal request
March 2011: Succeeded Bertrand Serlet as VP of Mac Software Engineering
August 2012: Promoted to SVP, reporting to Tim Cook — role expanded to all Apple platforms following Scott Forstall's departure
Owns: iOS, iPadOS, macOS, tvOS, watchOS, visionOS, and all software frameworks across Apple platforms
Also owns Apple Intelligence / on-device AI integration (2024-present)
Tim Cook has reportedly called him 'Superman' (L2: reported)

key_decisions_and_what_they_reveal.reveals: Willingness to replace a working language (Objective-C) entirely when the foundation is wrong; prioritizes long-term developer experience over short-term compatibility

key_decisions_and_what_they_reveal.decision: SwiftUI (announced 2019) — declarative UI across all Apple platforms
key_decisions_and_what_they_reveal.reveals: Platform unification through shared abstractions rather than shared code; one mental model, multiple implementations
Treats security as a binary property — a backdoor for the FBI is a backdoor for everyone; no 'good actor' exception in cryptographic security

key_decisions_and_what_they_reveal.decision: Private Cloud Compute architecture (2024) — on-device AI + cloud with verifiable privacy
key_decisions_and_what_they_reveal.reveals: Refuses the forced choice between AI capability and privacy; invests in engineering to have both; 'not even Apple can see your data' as a design goal

key_decisions_and_what_they_reveal.decision: 'We Don't Want to Build Sporks' — keeping iPad and Mac as distinct platforms (2025)
key_decisions_and_what_they_reveal.reveals: Resists pressure to converge platforms just because it's technically possible; believes interface metaphors matter and should match use context
Platform integrity is a security property, not a business model choice; the App Store review is a security layer, not just curation

key_decisions_and_what_they_reveal.decision: Delaying Apple Intelligence features that 'wouldn't meet customer expectations or Apple standards' (2025)
key_decisions_and_what_they_reveal.reveals: Willing to publicly admit delays rather than ship substandard features; quality as external commitment, not internal aspiration

documented_failures.specifics: Multiple observers documented 'an increase in defects and bugs within Apple's software products' under Federighi's decade of leadership (Wikipedia, L2). Apple Maps launch failure (2012, Scott Forstall's tenure, but Federighi inherited the repair). iCloud reliability issues. Several embarrassing iOS bugs.
documented_failures.response: Apple publicly acknowledged quality issues; reorganized some QA processes; Federighi did not publicly comment directly on the quality critiques

documented_failures.failure: Siri's persistent capability gap vs. competitors
documented_failures.specifics: Despite years of development, Siri significantly lagged behind Google Assistant, Alexa, and eventually ChatGPT in language understanding and task completion.
documented_failures.response: Publicly acknowledged the initial architecture was 'too limited' (2025); Apple Intelligence represents the architectural rethink; admitted beta testing doesn't provide 'the interaction and influence many users desire' (2022)

documented_failures.specifics: Personalized Siri features delayed significantly past announced timelines
documented_failures.response: Federighi explicitly stated features wouldn't ship until they met 'customer expectations or Apple standards' — framed delay as quality commitment, not failure

documented_positions_on_major_topics.privacy: Fundamental human right, not a feature; no tradeoff between capability and privacy; 'not even Apple' should be able to see user data
documented_positions_on_major_topics.security: Binary property — a backdoor for law enforcement is a backdoor for criminals; the App Store review is a security perimeter
documented_positions_on_major_topics.platform_identity: Mac and iPad serve fundamentally different use cases requiring different architectures — not building sporks
documented_positions_on_major_topics.ai_integration: On-device first; Private Cloud Compute for anything requiring server resources; no AI companions or engagement mechanics — 'here to help, not here for engagement'
documented_positions_on_major_topics.developer_experience: Swift: making the obvious path the safest; SwiftUI: one mental model across platforms; developer velocity comes from removing friction in the right places
documented_positions_on_major_topics.quality: Features ship only when they meet customer expectations; public delay is preferable to substandard release
